swiss_national_day.intro: Switzerland's National Day on August 1st commemorates the Federal Charter of 1291 — the symbolic founding act of the Confederation.
swiss_national_day.history: In August 1291 the founding cantons of Uri, Schwyz and Unterwalden formed an ‘eternal alliance' on the Rütli meadow against Habsburg rule. For a long time the day was only a regional celebration in some cantons; only in 1891, on its 600-year jubilee, was it introduced nationwide. Until 1994 it was only a ‘half holiday'; in a referendum the Swiss decided by a large majority to upgrade it to a full work-free public holiday.
swiss_national_day.traditions: The highlight is the evening federal celebration with speeches, traditional songs like the ‘Swiss Psalm' and bonfires on the mountains. Families gather on the Rütli, on Lake Lucerne or in their communities for celebrations. Customary elements include waving Swiss flags, ringing bells and lighting beacon fires on heights. Classics on the table: cervelat sausage from the grill and Swiss apple cake.
funFacts: The date ‘August 1st' comes from the Federal Charter, signed ‘in the first days of the month of August' 1291 — the exact day is an estimate.
On the Rütli meadow on Lake Lucerne the central federal celebration takes place each year — admission is free.
There are traditional ‘federal celebration candies' with the Swiss cross — a sweet emblem of every party.
Switzerland is one of few countries worldwide whose national date is set not by a battle but by a treaty document.
